sentiment.rationale: God commands Hosea to marry an unfaithful wife as a symbol of Israel's spiritual adultery and covenant betrayal.
A lawsuit against Israel for harlotry, yet ends with promises of restoration and a new betrothal in righteousness.
Hosea redeems his wife from slavery, symbolizing God's patient love despite Israel's unfaithfulness.
A devastating indictment of Israel's lack of knowledge, truth, and mercy, with priests leading the people astray.
Judgment pronounced against both Israel and Judah for pride and unfaithfulness to the covenant.
A call to return to the Lord who has torn and will heal, but Israel's goodness vanishes like the morning dew.
Israel is compared to a cake not turned, half-baked in devotion, mixing with nations and forgetting God.
Israel has sown the wind and shall reap the whirlwind; their idols and foreign alliances condemned.
Announces coming exile and barrenness as consequences of sin; the prophet himself is called a fool by the people.
Israel is an empty vine; calls to sow in righteousness and reap mercy, but warns of coming destruction.
God's tender love for Israel as a child is recalled, expressing anguish over their rebellion yet refusing to destroy them utterly.
Recalls Jacob's wrestling with God and calls Ephraim to repentance, exposing their deceit and reliance on foreign powers.
Severe warnings of destruction for continued idolatry, yet contains the messianic promise of ransoming from death.
A beautiful concluding invitation to return to the Lord, with promises of healing, love, and flourishing like a green fir tree.

missingCharacters.name: Gomer
missingCharacters.description: Daughter of Diblaim, wife of the prophet Hosea, taken as a symbol of Israel's unfaithfulness to God.

summaries.summary: God commands Hosea to marry Gomer, a woman of harlotry, and their three children are given symbolic names representing God's judgment on Israel.
God brings charges against Israel for spiritual adultery, threatening judgment, but promises future restoration and a new covenant of betrothal.
Hosea buys back his unfaithful wife, symbolizing God's willingness to redeem Israel after a period of discipline and separation.
God's controversy with Israel: no truth, mercy, or knowledge of God in the land; priests and people alike are condemned for idolatry and ignorance.
Judgment falls on priests, people, and kings of both Israel and Judah for pride and unfaithfulness; God will withdraw until they seek His face.
A call to return to the Lord who wounds and heals; God desires mercy and not sacrifice, and knowledge of God more than burnt offerings.
Israel is compared to a hot oven of conspiracy, a cake not turned, and a silly dove, mixing with heathen nations and forgetting God.
Israel has broken the covenant and transgressed the law; they have sown the wind and shall reap the whirlwind through foreign invasion.
Exile and barrenness are announced as punishment; Israel's corruption at Baal-peor is recalled, and Ephraim's glory shall fly away.
Israel is an empty vine bringing forth fruit for itself; the people are called to sow righteousness and reap mercy before destruction comes.
God recalls tenderly calling Israel as a child out of Egypt and expresses anguished love, refusing to completely destroy them despite their rebellion.
Ephraim's deceit is contrasted with Jacob's wrestling with God; the people are called to return to God and keep mercy and judgment.
Severe judgment for idolatry and ingratitude; yet God promises to ransom from death and redeem from the grave, declaring victory over death.
A final invitation to return with words of repentance; God promises to heal their backsliding and love them freely, making them flourish.

crossReferences.note: Paul quotes Hosea's promise that those called 'not my people' shall be called children of the living God.
Peter applies this restoration promise to Gentile believers who were once not a people but are now God's people.
Jesus quotes this verse twice, teaching the Pharisees that God desires mercy rather than sacrifice.
Matthew applies 'Out of Egypt have I called my son' to the return of Jesus from Egypt.
Paul quotes the triumph over death and the grave in his resurrection discourse.

verses.reason: God's shocking command for Hosea to marry a wife of whoredoms as a living parable of Israel's unfaithfulness.
A promise of everlasting betrothal in righteousness, judgment, lovingkindness, and mercy.
Hosea buys back his wife for fifteen pieces of silver, prefiguring divine redemption.
'My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge'—a warning about spiritual ignorance.
God withdraws until the people acknowledge their offence and seek His face.
'I desired mercy, and not sacrifice; and the knowledge of God more than burnt offerings'—quoted twice by Jesus.
'Ephraim is a cake not turned'—a vivid image of half-hearted devotion.
'They have sown the wind, and they shall reap the whirlwind'—a proverbial warning.
'Sow to yourselves in righteousness, reap in mercy; break up your fallow ground.'
'When Israel was a child, then I loved him, and called my son out of Egypt'—applied to Christ by Matthew.
A call to turn to God, keep mercy and judgment, and wait on God continually.
'I will ransom them from the power of the grave; I will redeem them from death'—a messianic resurrection promise.
'I will heal their backsliding, I will love them freely: for mine anger is turned away.'
